Cricket News

England Cricket 2025: The Best Seats at Lord's, The Oval, and Edgbaston

ByAnika Sharma|5 February 2025  ·  6 min read

Lord's Cricket Ground in St John's Wood is the spiritual home of cricket worldwide. (Getty Images)

Which end is best for watching Test cricket? Our ground-by-ground breakdown of the best viewing positions and how to book them.

Lord's Cricket Ground: Where to Sit for the Full Experience

Lord's is unlike any other cricket ground in the world, and choosing where to sit requires understanding its unique character. The Compton Stand offers the purest cricket view with the Pavilion directly behind the bowler's arm from the Pavilion End — ideal for reading line and length. The Grandstand and Edrich Stand provide a broader panoramic view at a slightly higher price point. The Warner Stand is popular for its central position but can be impacted by the famous Lord's slope which tilts the whole playing surface by 2.5 metres. Avoid the Nursery End upper tier if you prioritise an unobstructed view of the iconic Pavilion backdrop.

The Oval: Best Stands for Atmosphere and Visibility

The Kia Oval in Kennington, London is a ground where the atmosphere consistently outperforms expectations for a city venue. The OCS Stand runs along the pavilion side and offers some of the best sightlines in English cricket from the middle and upper tiers. For those who prefer a livelier atmosphere, the Bedser Stand opposite fills quickly with a knowledgeable crowd and is cheaper than the OCS equivalent. The most memorable seats in the ground are in the lower Pavilion, directly behind the bowler's arm — but these are members-only for the first three days of each Test.

Edgbaston: The Loudest Ground in England

Edgbaston in Birmingham has cemented its reputation as England's most vocal Test cricket venue, with the Eric Hollies Stand generating an atmosphere that commentators regularly compare to a football ground. Seats in the Eric Hollies Stand are the cheapest at the ground, extremely popular, and frequently sell out first — book as soon as they go on sale. For families, the Raglan Suite end offers covered seating and clearer sightlines without the boisterous element. The pavilion stands offer outstanding views down the pitch and are the choice of the ground's most serious cricket watchers.

How to Book England Cricket Tickets and Avoid Common Pitfalls

England home Test tickets are sold exclusively through the ECB and the host county club. Membership of the host county (Middlesex for Lord's, Surrey for The Oval, Warwickshire for Edgbaston) provides priority booking windows of up to four weeks before general sale — for major series such as the Ashes, this is often the only viable route to sought-after seats. General sale dates are announced via the ECB website in the autumn. On the secondary market, FootballTicketNet and Awin carry legitimate resale inventory, but premiums of 80–120% above face value are common for Ashes Tests. Prices tend to soften only in the final week before each Test day.
